lessons.title: What is pre-production?
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what pre-production means
I can name pre-production planning tools
I can choose a planning tool for a media product
I can create a piece of pre-production planning
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: pre-production · mood board · mind map · storyboard · script · visualisation. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; pens and a big sheet per group of 3–5. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — a new job. A client has built a home robot and wants a website. What should we ASK before we start? Land on the word pre-production = the planning stage. Pupils choose the meaning, then drag the three production stages into order.
   • Support: matching planning tool to what it is.  • Core: name a tool you’d use to plan a video.  • Challenge: difference between a mood board and a storyboard.
2. (10–20) I-do — the three stages: PRE-production (plan), PRODUCTION (make), POST-production (finish/release). Likely error: “pre-production is filming” → fix-words “pre-production is BEFORE we make anything.”
3. (20–30) Tour the tools with the source examples: mood board, storyboard, script, visualisation. Pupils sort jobs into pre/production/post on the activity worksheet.
4. (30–50) You-do — in groups, make a mind map for the robot website, then a storyboard for a short robot video. Photo both onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt with the headings, do not write it for them.” Likely error: writing a paragraph not boxes → fix-words “one box = one shot.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — which tool suits which media type? Self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Creating digital graphics
lessons.objectives:
I can describe raster and vector graphics
I can name file formats for each type
I can make a graphic using open-source software
I can explain copyright and Creative Commons
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: raster · vector · pixel · file format · open source · copyright · Creative Commons. TA: GIMP and Inkscape open for everyone; graphical assets shared. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — raster vs vector. Raster = pixels (a photo, goes blurry enlarged); vector = maths paths (stays sharp). Pupils choose which stays sharp, then sort file extensions into raster/vector on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: choose what a raster is made of.  • Core: define the difference.  • Challenge: which type a logo should be, and why.
2. (10–30) I-do → you-do — make a VECTOR logo in Inkscape (Fab Gadget). I-do the first shapes, then they follow the worksheet. Likely error: text not on the path → fix-words “select text AND circle first.”
3. (30–50) Make a RASTER poster in GIMP from supplied photos. Pupils drag the poster steps into order first. Stress LAYERS. Add the logo on top.
4. (40–50) Legal use — copyright vs Creative Commons. Multi-select: use a licensed image, credit the creator, credit the source. Misconception: “online means free” → fix-words “online does not mean free to reuse.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — raster or vector for each file extension; self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Creating digital video
lessons.objectives:
I can name camera angles used in video
I can describe video properties: FPS, compression, file format
I can use OpenShot to edit a short video
I can export a finished video file
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: camera angle · frames per second · compression · codec · container · transition · export. TA: OpenShot installed, video assets shared, headphones out. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — camera angles (close-up, long shot, high/low angle). Demo each with a hand as the camera. Pupils LABEL the parts of a video camera and match angles to descriptions on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: matching angle to description.  • Core: which angle makes a subject look weak (high angle).  • Challenge: why angles are chosen at the storyboard stage.
2. (10–25) Video properties — frames (flip-book), FPS, compression (smaller file, less quality), container vs codec. Teacher-played flip-book hook (motion only — play if it suits the class). Fill-in-the-blank on the activity worksheet.
3. (25–50) You-do — edit in OpenShot. I-do: import → drag a clip → one cut → one fade. Pupils order the editing steps, then build the short robot-mower video and keep the logo on top. Export as MP4.
   • Support: choose what FPS / a container is.  • Core: why compression is a trade-off.  • Challenge: how to keep the logo on screen the whole video. Likely error: not exporting → fix-words “save the project AND export the video.” TA: prompt, do not edit for them.
4. (50–55) Plenary — missing-words recap; self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Creating a multi-page website
lessons.objectives:
I can describe features of a good website
I can plan a multi-page website
I can build a multi-page website with HTML
I can add my own logo and video to the site
lessons.outline: Routine first. Vocabulary on board: navigation · call to action · hosting · SSL certificate · HTML · CSS. TA: text editor installed, template folder shared, sticky notes out. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud. 1. (0–10) Starter — what makes a website good? Sort features into FRONT END (what visitors see: home page, navigation, logo, call to action) and BACK END (server: hosting, SSL certificate, CMS) on the starter worksheet. • Support: choose what navigation / a call to action is. • Core: what a call to action is and why a business wants one. • Challenge: why a site taking payments needs SSL. 2. (10–20) I-do — HTML = content/structure (tags in < >); CSS = design. Show one link and one tag. Keep it light — they edit a template, not write from scratch. 3. (20–50) You-do — build from the template: copy the folder, make the four pages, change headings and text, check in a browser. Pupils order the build steps, then add their Lesson 2 logo and Lesson 3 video. Likely error: filename not exactly logo.png → fix-words “the code looks for logo.png exactly.” TA: prompt, do not type for them. 4. (50–55) Plenary — true/false on website features; self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Planning your digital media artefact
lessons.objectives:
I can read a client brief and choose one
I can choose a media artefact to make
I can plan the artefact with my group
I can use the rubric to aim for a higher level
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: digital media artefact · client brief · stock materials · delegation · collaboration · rubric. TA: groups of 4–5 set; the rubric, briefs and storyboard templates ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — key words. Digital media artefact, client brief, delegation, rubric. Pupils match each word to its meaning on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: matching word to meaning.  • Core: what a client brief is and why it comes first.  • Challenge: why to document the reasons for choices.
2. (10–20) Introduce the marking rubric — walk through one row so they know what “excelling” looks like. They are graded against this in Lesson 7.
3. (20–50) You-do — in groups, read the four briefs (game app, farming robots, medical AI, driverless truck), choose ONE brief and ONE artefact (poster / video / website), pick pre-production tools, and delegate jobs. Pupils order the planning steps and rate their readiness on a scale.
   • Support: choose what to read first / what delegation means.  • Core: which brief they chose and why.  • Challenge: how to reach the top rubric level on one row. TA: prompt quieter members to take a real job.
4. (50–55) Plenary — review another group’s plan; self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Homework: finish the planning.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Producing your digital media artefact
lessons.objectives:
I can work in my team and do my delegated job
I can help make our digital media artefact
I can use the rubric to check our work
I can name one thing to improve and how
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: collaboration · delegation · teamwork · communication · documentation. TA: software ready; the rubric out per group; cameras/devices if filming. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — good teamwork. Collaborate = work together; delegate = share the jobs. Multi-select good team behaviours; reset who is doing which job today.
   • Support: choose what collaboration / delegation means.  • Core: name a job and who is doing it.  • Challenge: a fair way to help a teammate who is behind.
2. (10–20) Check the rubric — rate each row on a scale, pick ONE thing to improve today.
3. (20–50) You-do — make the artefact. Roles: documentation/presentation lead, two artefact leads (logo + poster/video/site), support sourcing stock materials. Remind: licence-check and credit any stock images. TA: prompt, do not take over. Likely error: not recording design choices → fix-words “write down WHY, as you go.”
4. (50–55) Plenary — rate the rubric again; what moved up, what is next? Self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Can be finished as homework / catch-up.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Presenting your digital media artefact and end-of-unit quiz
lessons.objectives:
I can present my work and explain my design choices
I can evaluate what worked and what to improve
I can assess another group fairly using the rubric
I can answer questions about the whole unit
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: peer assessment · rubric · weighting · reflection · evaluate. TA: a rubric per pupil per group; presentation facilities ready; the end-of-unit quiz worksheet ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — peer assessment. You grade the WORK against the rubric, fairly and kindly. Pupils choose what peer assessment means on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: choose what good feedback / “evaluate” means.  • Core: one thing they will say to explain a design choice.  • Challenge: why the teacher’s grade is weighted more.
2. (10–35) Group presentations — each group shows the artefact, the process, and their design choices. Non-presenters complete a rubric per group. Prompt any group that misses a rubric area.
3. (35–52) End-of-unit quiz and evaluation (the summative knowledge check, exam-style but supported): match pre-production tools, single-choice across pre-production/graphics/video/websites, multi-select Creative Commons rules, fill-in FPS/compression, then evaluate what worked and what they’d change.
   • Support: recognition single-choices.  • Core: why a logo is a vector.  • Challenge: two audience-fit design choices for a brief.
4. (52–55) Recap the unit journey (plan → create graphics/video/website → plan/produce/present a real artefact). Self-tick the 4 “I can…”.
Differentiation: the starter and quiz S/C/C sit on the SAME tasks at three depths; the presentation/peer-assessment is shared. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
